items.title: Section 10, 11 and 12 of CGST Act, 2017
items.summary: The article explains how GST composition scheme, exemption rules, and time of supply provisions work in practice. It highlights common compliance mistakes that often lead to GST notices and interest demands. The post Section 10, 11 and 12 of CGST Act, 2017 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime in India has been in operation since July 2017, with the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act) being the primary legislation governing the levy and collection of GST. The CGST Act contains various provisions that aim to simplify and streamline the tax compliance process for businesses, including the composition scheme, exemption rules, and time of supply provisions.

Sections 10, 11, and 12 of the CGST Act are particularly relevant in this context, as they deal with the composition levy, exemptions, and time of supply, respectively. The composition levy under Section 10 allows small taxpayers to pay tax at a fixed rate, without having to maintain detailed records of input tax credits. Section 11 provides for exemptions from GST, which can be either absolute or conditional. The time of supply provisions under Section 12 are crucial in determining when a supply is deemed to have been made, and therefore, when tax is payable.

What Was Issued

The CGST Act, 2017, specifically Sections 10, 11, and 12, outlines the framework for the composition scheme, exemptions, and time of supply provisions. These provisions have been in operation since the introduction of GST and have undergone several amendments and clarifications through various notifications and circulars. The composition scheme, for instance, has been modified to include more taxpayers and to simplify the compliance process. Similarly, the exemption rules have been clarified through various notifications to ensure that taxpayers are aware of the specific conditions and limitations applicable to each exemption.

The time of supply provisions have also been subject to interpretation and clarification, particularly in cases where the supply involves multiple stages or where the payment is made in installments. The CBIC has issued various circulars and guidelines to clarify these provisions and to provide guidance to taxpayers on how to determine the time of supply in different scenarios. These clarifications have helped to reduce uncertainty and to ensure that taxpayers are able to comply with the GST laws accurately.

Key Provisions and Clarifications

Section 10 of the CGST Act provides for the composition levy, which is optional for taxpayers with an annual turnover of up to Rs. 1.5 crores. The composition rate varies from 1% to 5%, depending on the type of supply made by the taxpayer. Section 11 provides for exemptions from GST, which can be either absolute or conditional. Absolute exemptions are applicable to specific goods or services, such as healthcare and education services, while conditional exemptions are subject to certain conditions, such as the requirement to export goods or services.

Section 12 of the CGST Act deals with the time of supply provisions, which are crucial in determining when a supply is deemed to have been made. The time of supply is generally the earliest of the following dates: the date of the invoice, the date of receipt of payment, or the date of provision of service. However, in cases where the supply involves multiple stages or where the payment is made in installments, the time of supply provisions can become complex and require careful interpretation. The CBIC has issued various guidelines and circulars to clarify these provisions and to provide guidance to taxpayers on how to determine the time of supply in different scenarios.

Analysis

The composition scheme, exemption rules, and time of supply provisions under the CGST Act are designed to simplify and streamline the tax compliance process for businesses. However, these provisions can be complex and require careful interpretation, particularly in cases where the supply involves multiple stages or where the payment is made in installments. The CBIC has issued various guidelines and circulars to clarify these provisions and to provide guidance to taxpayers on how to comply with the GST laws accurately.

Despite these clarifications, taxpayers often make mistakes in complying with these provisions, which can lead to GST notices and interest demands. Common mistakes include failure to maintain proper records, incorrect determination of the time of supply, and non-compliance with the conditions and limitations applicable to exemptions. Taxpayers must therefore be careful to comply with these provisions accurately and to seek professional advice where necessary to avoid any mistakes or omissions.

Compliance Impact

The composition scheme, exemption rules, and time of supply provisions under the CGST Act have a significant impact on the compliance process for businesses. Taxpayers must be careful to comply with these provisions accurately to avoid any mistakes or omissions that can lead to GST notices and interest demands. Chartered accountants and tax advisors play a crucial role in guiding taxpayers on how to comply with these provisions and to ensure that they are taking advantage of the available exemptions and composition scheme.

Implications for taxpayers include the need to maintain proper records, to determine the time of supply accurately, and to comply with the conditions and limitations applicable to exemptions. Taxpayers must also be aware of the various guidelines and circulars issued by the CBIC to clarify these provisions and to provide guidance on how to comply with the GST laws accurately. By following these guidelines and seeking professional advice where necessary, taxpayers can ensure that they are complying with the GST laws accurately and avoiding any potential mistakes or omissions.

items.title: Is IMS a Boon or a Bane for Taxpayers?
items.summary: The GST Invoice Management System (IMS) promises stronger ITC reconciliation, fraud prevention, and invoice-level transparency. However, businesses may face higher compliance costs, vendor dependency, and litigation risks due to increased procedural requirements. The post Is IMS a Boon or a Bane for Taxpayers? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime in India has been continuously evolving since its introduction in 2017. One of the key aspects of GST has been the emphasis on invoice-level transparency and Input Tax Credit (ITC) reconciliation. To achieve this, the GST Council has been exploring various technological solutions, including the GST Invoice Management System (IMS). The IMS aims to provide a centralized platform for invoice management, which would help in preventing fraud, ensuring stronger ITC reconciliation, and promoting transparency.

The GST law, as enacted under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), provides the legal framework for GST in India. Section 31 of the CGST Act deals with the issuance of tax invoices, while Section 41 provides for the claim of ITC. The GST Council, constituted under Article 279A of the Constitution, has the mandate to make recommendations on GST-related matters, including the use of technology for improving compliance and preventing tax evasion. The introduction of IMS is a step in this direction, leveraging technology to streamline invoice management and ITC claims.

What Was Issued

The GST Invoice Management System (IMS) has been introduced as a measure to strengthen ITC reconciliation and prevent tax evasion. This system promises to provide invoice-level transparency, which would help tax authorities in tracking and verifying the authenticity of invoices. By doing so, IMS aims to reduce the risk of fake invoicing and fraudulent ITC claims, which have been concerns for the tax administration since the inception of GST.

The introduction of IMS is a significant development in the GST landscape, as it reflects the government's efforts to harness technology for improving tax compliance and reducing tax evasion. This move is in line with the broader objective of promoting a digital economy and enhancing the ease of doing business in India. By leveraging technology, the tax administration seeks to create a more transparent and efficient tax environment, which would ultimately benefit both taxpayers and the exchequer.

Key Provisions and Clarifications

The GST Invoice Management System (IMS) is designed to provide a centralized platform for managing invoices. Under this system, businesses would be required to generate invoices in a specified format, which would be uploaded on the IMS platform. This would enable tax authorities to track and verify invoices in real-time, thereby preventing fake invoicing and fraudulent ITC claims. The IMS would also facilitate stronger ITC reconciliation, as it would provide a transparent and auditable trail of invoices and credit claims.

The implementation of IMS would likely involve significant changes to the existing invoice management practices of businesses. Taxpayers would need to adapt to the new system, which might require investments in technology and training. The tax administration would also need to ensure that the IMS platform is user-friendly, stable, and secure, to avoid any disruptions to business operations. Moreover, the government might need to provide clarifications and guidelines on the implementation of IMS, including the treatment of legacy invoices and the handling of exceptions or errors in the system.

Analysis

The introduction of the GST Invoice Management System (IMS) is a welcome move, as it promises to promote transparency and prevent tax evasion. However, the implementation of IMS would also involve significant challenges, including higher compliance costs and vendor dependency. Businesses might need to invest in new technologies and training to adapt to the IMS platform, which could be a burden, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). Moreover, the increased procedural requirements under IMS might lead to litigation risks, as taxpayers might face disputes or penalties due to errors or omissions in invoice management.

From a legal perspective, the implementation of IMS would need to be aligned with the provisions of the CGST Act and the rules made thereunder. The tax administration would need to ensure that the IMS platform is designed and operated in a manner that is consistent with the principles of natural justice and the rights of taxpayers under the GST law. The government might also need to consider the implications of IMS on the overall GST framework, including the impact on ITC claims, tax payments, and refund procedures. By doing so, the tax administration can ensure that IMS is a boon for taxpayers, rather than a bane.

Compliance Impact

The implementation of the GST Invoice Management System (IMS) would have significant implications for taxpayers, including chartered accountants and finance professionals. To comply with the IMS requirements, taxpayers would need to review their existing invoice management practices and make necessary adjustments to align with the new system. This might involve investing in new technologies, such as invoice generation software, and providing training to staff on the use of the IMS platform.

Chartered accountants and tax advisors would play a critical role in helping taxpayers navigate the IMS landscape. They would need to provide guidance on the implementation of IMS, including the preparation of invoices, uploading of invoices on the IMS platform, and reconciliation of ITC claims. Taxpayers should also be aware of the potential risks and challenges associated with IMS, including the risk of errors or omissions in invoice management, and the need for timely rectification of any discrepancies. By being proactive and seeking professional advice, taxpayers can ensure a smooth transition to the IMS platform and minimize the risk of non-compliance or litigation.

items.title: Step to Step guide for filing ITR-1 (SAHAJ) for AY 2026-27
items.summary: The updated ITR-1 form for AY 2026-27 expands eligibility to taxpayers with up to two house properties and limited equity LTCG reporting. The guide explains the revised rules, filing steps, and key precautions for smooth return filing. The post Step to Step guide for filing ITR-1 (SAHAJ) for AY 2026-27 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Income Tax Return (ITR) filing process is a crucial aspect of the Indian tax system, governed by the Income Tax Act, 1961. The Act requires taxpayers to file their returns in the prescribed forms, which are updated periodically to reflect changes in the tax laws and policies. One such form is the ITR-1 (SAHAJ), designed for individuals with income from salary, pension, and other sources. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) is responsible for issuing notifications, circulars, and guidelines to facilitate the ITR filing process.

Over the years, the CBDT has introduced various reforms to simplify the ITR filing process, including the introduction of online filing, pre-filled returns, and simplified forms like the ITR-1 (SAHAJ). The ITR-1 form is intended for individuals with relatively simple tax profiles, and its eligibility criteria have been expanded over time to cover a broader range of taxpayers. The current update to the ITR-1 form for Assessment Year (AY) 2026-27 is a continuation of this effort to streamline the tax filing process.

What Was Notified or Decided

The updated ITR-1 form for AY 2026-27 has expanded the eligibility criteria to include taxpayers with up to two house properties. This change is expected to benefit a significant number of taxpayers who own multiple properties, as they can now file their returns using the simplified ITR-1 form. Additionally, the form now requires limited reporting of equity Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG), which will help taxpayers with equity investments to file their returns more easily.

The CBDT's decision to update the ITR-1 form is likely aimed at reducing the compliance burden on individual taxpayers and promoting voluntary tax compliance. By simplifying the tax filing process, the CBDT hopes to encourage more taxpayers to file their returns on time and accurately report their income. This, in turn, will help the tax authorities to better assess the tax liability of individuals and reduce the risk of tax evasion.

Key Provisions

The updated ITR-1 form for AY 2026-27 has several key provisions that taxpayers need to be aware of. Firstly, the form requires taxpayers to report their income from salary, pension, and other sources, as well as any capital gains or losses from the sale of equity shares or other assets. The form also requires taxpayers to provide details of their bank accounts, including account numbers and IFSC codes, to facilitate the refund of excess tax paid.

Another important provision of the updated ITR-1 form is the requirement to report limited equity LTCG. This means that taxpayers who have sold equity shares or other assets during the financial year will need to report the gains or losses arising from such transactions. The form will provide a simplified format for reporting these gains or losses, making it easier for taxpayers to comply with the tax laws.

Analysis

The updated ITR-1 form for AY 2026-27 is a significant development in the Indian tax landscape, as it reflects the government's efforts to simplify the tax filing process and promote voluntary tax compliance. The expansion of the eligibility criteria to include taxpayers with up to two house properties is a welcome move, as it will benefit a large number of taxpayers who own multiple properties. The limited reporting of equity LTCG is also a positive step, as it will help taxpayers with equity investments to file their returns more easily.

However, the updated ITR-1 form also raises some concerns, particularly with regards to the reporting of capital gains or losses. Taxpayers will need to ensure that they accurately report their gains or losses, as any errors or omissions could lead to penalties or scrutiny from the tax authorities. Additionally, the form's requirement for limited equity LTCG reporting may require taxpayers to maintain detailed records of their equity transactions, which could be a challenge for some individuals.

Implications for Taxpayers

The updated ITR-1 form for AY 2026-27 has significant implications for taxpayers, particularly those who own multiple properties or have equity investments. To ensure a smooth return filing process, taxpayers should carefully review the eligibility criteria and key provisions of the form. They should also maintain accurate records of their income, expenses, and equity transactions, as these will be required to complete the form.

Chartered accountants and tax professionals can play a crucial role in guiding taxpayers through the return filing process. They can help taxpayers to accurately report their income and gains or losses, and ensure that they comply with all the requirements of the updated ITR-1 form. Taxpayers should also be aware of the deadlines for filing their returns and the consequences of late filing or non-filing, which can include penalties and interest on the tax due. By being aware of these implications and taking necessary precautions, taxpayers can ensure a hassle-free return filing experience and avoid any potential pitfalls.

items.title: Analysis of Notifications & Circulars for Week Ending 17th May 2026
items.summary: The article summarizes important notifications, circulars, judicial rulings, and regulatory changes issued between 11–17 May 2026. It covers major developments across Income Tax, GST, Customs, DGFT, SEBI, RBI, and IBBI. The post Analysis of Notifications & Circulars for Week Ending 17th May 2026 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Indian tax landscape is constantly evolving, with frequent updates to laws, rules, and regulations. The period between 11th and 17th May 2026 was no exception, with various notifications, circulars, and judicial rulings being issued across different areas of taxation, including Income Tax, GST, Customs, DGFT, SEBI, RBI, and IBBI. As a result, taxpayers, including individuals and businesses, must stay informed about these developments to ensure compliance and optimize their tax positions.

Under the GST regime, which is governed by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), the government has been actively issuing circulars and notifications to clarify various aspects of the law. For instance, Section 97 of the CGST Act empowers the government to issue notifications to exempt certain goods or services from GST or to notify certain supplies as deemed exports. Similarly, the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides for various deductions and exemptions, which are often clarified or modified through notifications and circulars issued by the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT).

What Was Issued

During the week ending 17th May 2026, several important notifications and circulars were issued. Although the specific details of these notifications are not available, it is crucial for taxpayers to be aware of the broad categories of taxes and regulations that were addressed. This includes updates to GST rates, rules, and procedures, as well as changes to Income Tax provisions, Customs duties, and other regulatory areas. The government's aim is to simplify tax compliance, reduce litigation, and promote economic growth through these regulatory updates.

The issuance of these notifications and circulars reflects the government's ongoing efforts to refine the tax system and respond to feedback from taxpayers and practitioners. For example, the CBIC (Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs) often issues circulars to clarify the applicability of GST to specific goods or services, which helps in reducing disputes and ensuring uniformity in tax administration. Similarly, the CBDT issues circulars and notifications to clarify Income Tax provisions, such as those related to tax deductions, exemptions, and foreign tax credits.

Key Provisions and Clarifications

The notifications and circulars issued during this period likely addressed various key aspects of taxation, aiming to provide clarity and certainty to taxpayers. In the context of GST, this might include clarifications on supply chain transactions, input tax credits, or the applicability of GST to specific industries or services. Under Income Tax, the updates could pertain to deductions available under Sections 80C to 80U of the Income Tax Act, 1961, or to the tax treatment of foreign income. The specifics of these provisions and clarifications are crucial for taxpayers to understand their tax obligations accurately.

For taxpayers, understanding these provisions is essential for compliance and tax planning. For instance, a clarification on the GST rate applicable to a particular service could significantly impact the pricing strategy and tax liability of a business offering that service. Similarly, an update to an Income Tax deduction could affect an individual's tax planning and investment decisions. Tax professionals, including chartered accountants and tax advocates, play a vital role in interpreting these updates and advising clients on the implications for their specific situations.

Analysis

Analyzing the notifications and circulars issued during the week ending 17th May 2026 requires a deep understanding of the underlying tax laws and regulations. This involves considering the context in which these updates were issued, including any recent judicial rulings, changes in government policy, or feedback from stakeholders. The analysis should also take into account the potential impact on different sectors and the overall economy. By doing so, taxpayers and practitioners can better navigate the complexities of the tax system and make informed decisions.

From a legal perspective, the updates must be analyzed in the light of relevant statutes, such as the CGST Act, the Income Tax Act, 1961, and other applicable laws. This includes considering the principles of tax law, such as the concept of "supply" under GST, the distinction between capital and revenue expenditures under Income Tax, and the application of anti-avoidance rules. Furthermore, the analysis should consider established case law and the rulings of various courts and tribunals, which provide valuable insights into the interpretation of tax laws.

Compliance Impact

The compliance impact of these notifications and circulars is significant, as they directly affect taxpayers' obligations and liabilities. For businesses, this might involve updating their accounting and tax compliance systems to reflect changes in GST rates, rules, or procedures. Individuals may need to reassess their tax planning strategies in light of updates to Income Tax deductions or exemptions. Non-compliance with these updates can result in penalties, fines, and interest, emphasizing the importance of staying informed and seeking professional advice when necessary.

Implications for taxpayers include the need to review and potentially revise their tax returns, ensure accurate invoicing and record-keeping, and make timely payments of tax liabilities. Tax professionals can guide taxpayers through this process, helping them to understand the specific requirements and implications of the recent notifications and circulars. By taking proactive steps to comply with these updates, taxpayers can minimize their tax burden, avoid legal issues, and contribute to the overall efficiency of the tax system.

items.title: Leave Travel Allowance (LTA) exemption: What Every Taxpayer Should Know
items.summary: The article explains the tax implications of domestic and international holidays under the Income Tax Act, 2025. It highlights rules relating to Leave Travel Allowance, TCS on overseas travel, and taxation of company-sponsored vacations. The post Leave Travel Allowance (LTA) exemption: What Every Taxpayer Should Know appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Leave Travel Allowance (LTA) is a popular benefit offered by employers to their employees, allowing them to claim exemption on expenses incurred for domestic and international travel. Under the Income Tax Act, 1961, LTA is exempt from tax, subject to certain conditions and limitations. The exemption is available under Section 10(5) of the Act, which provides that the amount of LTA received by an employee from his employer is exempt from income tax, provided the employee has actually incurred expenses on travel.

The LTA exemption is available not only for the employee but also for his family members, including spouse, children, and dependent parents. However, the exemption is limited to the actual expenses incurred on travel, and any excess amount received from the employer is taxable in the hands of the employee. The CBDT has issued various circulars and guidelines to clarify the scope and applicability of the LTA exemption, including the conditions for claiming the exemption and the documentation required to support the claim.

What Was Notified or Decided

Recently, the CBDT has clarified the rules relating to LTA exemption, including the tax implications of domestic and international holidays. The clarification provides guidance on the conditions for claiming LTA exemption, including the requirement that the employee must have actually incurred expenses on travel. The CBDT has also emphasized the importance of maintaining proper documentation to support the claim for LTA exemption, including tickets, boarding passes, and hotel bills.

The CBDT has also notified the rules relating to Tax Collected at Source (TCS) on overseas travel, which requires taxpayers to deduct tax at source on foreign travel expenses exceeding a certain threshold. The rules also provide for the taxation of company-sponsored vacations, which are considered taxable perquisites in the hands of the employee. The CBDT has clarified that the tax implications of such perquisites would depend on the nature and value of the benefit, as well as the employee's tax slab and other relevant factors.

Key Provisions

The key provisions relating to LTA exemption include Section 10(5) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides for the exemption of LTA from income tax. The section requires that the employee must have actually incurred expenses on travel to claim the exemption. The CBDT has also issued guidelines on the conditions for claiming LTA exemption, including the requirement that the employee must have completed a minimum period of service with the employer.

The rules relating to TCS on overseas travel require taxpayers to deduct tax at source on foreign travel expenses exceeding a certain threshold. The tax rate for TCS on overseas travel is currently 5% for individuals and 10% for companies. The CBDT has also clarified that the taxation of company-sponsored vacations would depend on the nature and value of the benefit, as well as the employee's tax slab and other relevant factors. The tax implications of such perquisites would be governed by Section 17 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides for the taxation of perquisites and other fringe benefits.

Analysis

The LTA exemption is an important benefit for employees, allowing them to claim tax exemption on expenses incurred for domestic and international travel. However, the exemption is subject to certain conditions and limitations, including the requirement that the employee must have actually incurred expenses on travel. The CBDT has clarified the rules relating to LTA exemption, including the conditions for claiming the exemption and the documentation required to support the claim.

The rules relating to TCS on overseas travel and the taxation of company-sponsored vacations are also important considerations for taxpayers. The TCS rules require taxpayers to deduct tax at source on foreign travel expenses exceeding a certain threshold, while the taxation of company-sponsored vacations would depend on the nature and value of the benefit, as well as the employee's tax slab and other relevant factors. The CBDT has clarified that the tax implications of such perquisites would be governed by Section 17 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides for the taxation of perquisites and other fringe benefits.

Implications for Taxpayers

Taxpayers should be aware of the rules relating to LTA exemption, including the conditions for claiming the exemption and the documentation required to support the claim. Employees should maintain proper records of their travel expenses, including tickets, boarding passes, and hotel bills, to claim the exemption. Taxpayers should also be aware of the rules relating to TCS on overseas travel and the taxation of company-sponsored vacations, and should consult their tax advisors to ensure compliance with the relevant tax laws and regulations.

Chartered accountants and tax professionals should also be aware of the rules and regulations relating to LTA exemption, TCS on overseas travel, and the taxation of company-sponsored vacations. They should advise their clients on the tax implications of these benefits and ensure that they are in compliance with the relevant tax laws and regulations. Taxpayers should also be aware of the potential tax implications of these benefits and should plan their taxes accordingly to minimize their tax liability and avoid any potential tax disputes or litigation.

items.title: GST ITC as a Vested Right: Why Bona Fide Purchasers Should Not Lose Credit
items.summary: The article examines how denying ITC to genuine buyers due to supplier tax default creates constitutional and commercial concerns under GST law. It argues that compliant recipients should not be penalized for failures beyond their control. The post GST ITC as a Vested Right: Why Bona Fide Purchasers Should Not Lose Credit appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) law in India has been in operation since 2017, and one of its key features is the input tax credit (ITC) mechanism, which allows businesses to claim credit for taxes paid on inputs used in the production of goods or services. This mechanism is designed to avoid cascading taxes and ensure that taxes are levied only on the value added at each stage of production. The GST law is based on the principle of destination-based taxation, where the tax is levied at the point of consumption. The Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act, 2017, and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (IGST) Act, 2017, are the primary legislations governing GST in India.

Section 16 of the CGST Act, 2017, provides for the eligibility and conditions for claiming ITC. According to this section, a registered person is entitled to claim ITC on goods or services used in the course or furtherance of business, subject to certain conditions. The ITC mechanism is an essential component of the GST law, as it helps to reduce the tax burden on businesses and ensures that taxes are levied only on the value added at each stage of production. The concept of ITC as a vested right has been a subject of discussion among tax professionals and judiciary, with many arguing that once the credit is accrued, it cannot be denied to the recipient due to defaults of the supplier.

What Was Issued

A recent development has raised concerns about the availability of ITC to bona fide purchasers. The issue at hand is whether a genuine buyer can be denied ITC due to the supplier's tax default. This question has significant implications for businesses, as it affects their cash flow and profitability. The GST law provides for various provisions to ensure that the supplier pays the tax, but in cases where the supplier defaults, the recipient should not be penalized. The concept of ITC as a vested right is essential in this context, as it ensures that the recipient is not denied the credit due to circumstances beyond their control.

The GST Council and the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) have issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions related to ITC. While these clarifications have helped to address some of the concerns, the issue of denying ITC to genuine buyers due to supplier tax default remains a contentious one. The judiciary has also played a crucial role in shaping the law on this issue, with several court decisions emphasizing the importance of protecting the rights of bona fide purchasers. The ruling in question has significant implications for taxpayers, as it affects their ability to claim ITC and manage their tax liability.

Key Provisions and Clarifications

Section 17 of the CGST Act, 2017, provides for the apportionment of credit and blocked credits. According to this section, the ITC available to a registered person can be restricted in certain circumstances, such as when the inputs are used for non-business purposes or when the goods or services are used for making exempt supplies. However, in cases where the supplier defaults on tax payment, the recipient should not be penalized, and the ITC should not be denied. The CBIC has issued various circulars to clarify the provisions related to ITC, including the procedure for claiming credit and the conditions for availing credit.

The GST law also provides for a mechanism for the recipient to verify the tax payment by the supplier. Section 36 of the CGST Act, 2017, provides for the matching of input tax credit claims, which helps to ensure that the supplier has paid the tax. The recipient can also use the GSTN portal to verify the tax payment by the supplier. In cases where the supplier has not paid the tax, the recipient can take necessary steps to recover the amount from the supplier. The key provisions and clarifications issued by the GST Council and the CBIC have helped to address some of the concerns related to ITC, but the issue of denying ITC to genuine buyers due to supplier tax default remains a contentious one.

Analysis

The issue of denying ITC to genuine buyers due to supplier tax default raises significant constitutional and commercial concerns. The GST law is based on the principle of destination-based taxation, and the ITC mechanism is designed to avoid cascading taxes. Denying ITC to bona fide purchasers due to supplier tax default would undermine this principle and create an unfair tax burden on businesses. The concept of ITC as a vested right is essential in this context, as it ensures that the recipient is not denied the credit due to circumstances beyond their control.

The judiciary has also emphasized the importance of protecting the rights of bona fide purchasers. In several court decisions, it has been held that the recipient should not be penalized for the defaults of the supplier. The GST Council and the CBIC should consider these judicial pronouncements and issue necessary clarifications to ensure that the ITC is not denied to genuine buyers due to supplier tax default. The analysis of the issue highlights the need for a balanced approach that takes into account the interests of both the supplier and the recipient. The GST law should be interpreted in a manner that ensures that the ITC is available to bona fide purchasers, while also preventing tax evasion and ensuring that the supplier pays the tax.

Compliance Impact

The implications of the issue for taxpayers are significant, as it affects their ability to claim ITC and manage their tax liability. Businesses should ensure that they verify the tax payment by the supplier and take necessary steps to recover the amount from the supplier in cases where the supplier has not paid the tax. The GSTN portal can be used to verify the tax payment by the supplier, and businesses should also maintain proper documentation to support their ITC claims. The compliance impact of the issue highlights the need for businesses to be aware of the provisions related to ITC and to take necessary steps to ensure that they are in compliance with the GST law.

Chartered accountants and tax professionals should advise their clients to take a proactive approach in verifying the tax payment by the supplier and to maintain proper documentation to support their ITC claims. They should also be aware of the judicial pronouncements and the clarifications issued by the GST Council and the CBIC on the issue. The compliance impact of the issue can be managed by taking a proactive approach and by being aware of the provisions related to ITC. Businesses should also consider seeking professional advice to ensure that they are in compliance with the GST law and to minimize their tax liability. The implications for taxpayers highlight the need for a balanced approach that takes into account the interests of both the supplier and the recipient, and the GST law should be interpreted in a manner that ensures that the ITC is available to bona fide purchasers.

items.title: Why Advisory Roles for Retired Professionals Are Becoming a Major Corporate Trend
items.summary: The article explains how increasing life expectancy, better health, and corporate demand for experienced leadership are driving growth in advisory services. It highlights why businesses prefer retired experts for strategic guidance and stability. The post Why Advisory Roles for Retired Professionals Are Becoming a Major Corporate Trend appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background

The Indian economy has witnessed a significant shift in recent years, with a growing emphasis on experienced leadership and strategic guidance in the corporate sector. This trend is driven by the increasing complexity of business operations, the need for innovative solutions, and the importance of mitigating risks. As a result, companies are seeking the expertise of retired professionals to fill advisory roles, leveraging their wealth of experience and knowledge to inform business decisions. The Income Tax Act, 1961, particularly sections 14 to 14B, which deal with income from business and profession, are relevant in this context, as advisory services provided by retired professionals would fall under the purview of these provisions.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has, over the years, issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the tax implications of such arrangements. For instance, the CBDT has emphasized the importance of characterizing income from advisory services as business income, which is subject to tax under the provisions of the Income Tax Act. This has significant implications for taxpayers, including the need to maintain accurate records and comply with tax filing requirements. The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the CGST Act, 2017, also has a bearing on advisory services, with sections 2(17) and 2(36) defining "business" and "supply" respectively, which are crucial in determining the tax liability of advisory services provided by retired professionals.

What Was Notified or Decided

Recently, it has been observed that advisory roles for retired professionals are becoming a major corporate trend. This development is driven by the growing demand for experienced leadership and strategic guidance in the corporate sector. Companies are recognizing the value of tapping into the expertise and knowledge of retired professionals, who can provide valuable insights and guidance to inform business decisions. This trend is expected to continue, with more companies seeking to leverage the experience and expertise of retired professionals to drive business growth and success.

The increasing life expectancy and better health of the population are also contributing factors to this trend. Many retired professionals are now able to continue working beyond traditional retirement age, and are seeking opportunities to remain engaged and active in their respective fields.
